coredata.dc:title: A strategy for the accurate detection of glucose in human serum based on the IFE effect of up-transformed nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Tianlu
Li, Yangqing
Zhang, Xuecheng
Lyu, Haixia
Xie, Zenghong
coredata.dc:description: Here, the inner filter effect (IFE) was applied to construct a “turn off” mode up-conversion fluorescence and colorimetric dual-reading detection platform consisting of the chromogenic substance p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and NaYF4:Yb/Er@NaYF4 core–shell up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) for the detection of glucose and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with high sensitivity and selectivity. In this strategy, hydrogen peroxide is produced in the process of glucose oxidation by catalyzing glucose oxidase (GOx). PPD is oxidized by H2O2 under the action of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), resulting in the transformation into its oxidation product PPDox, whose absorption peak coincides with the emission peak of UCNP at 540 nm, which can significantly quench the fluorescence of UCNP. The quenching efficiency is related to PPDox production, so the relationship between the glucose content and the fluorescence changes of UCNP can be established to realize the sensitive detection of glucose. UCNPs modified by –COOH and the oxidation product of PPD were negatively charged, which increased the distance between donor and recipient due to electrostatic repulsion. It was not conducive to the occurrence of distance-dependent fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which was also verified by fluorescence lifetime test. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the linear ranges of hydrogen peroxide and glucose were 2.5 ∼ 50 and 5 ∼ 80 μmol/L with the detection limit (LOD) of 0.32 and 0.83 μmol/L, respectively, which were lower than of some reported approaches. Furthermore, the system could be applied to the detection of glucose in actual serum sample and the results were consistency of commercial glucose meters which indicated that the protocol was reliable and potential applications for clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Upconversion nanoparticles
Fluorescence turn-off and colorimetric assay
Inner filter effect
p-Phenylenediamine
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of glucose in real samples
Glucose monitoring in real serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology program of Fujian Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21307013), Science and Technology program of Fujian Province (2019Y0003) and Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2015J01044).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of UCNPs for monitoring blood glucose.
FT-IR (A), XRD (B), SEM (C) and TEM (D) of UCNPs.
Fluorescence spectrum (A) and UV–vis absorption spectrum (B) of UCNPs in the different system.
UV–vis absorption spectrum (A)(B), zeta potential (C) and fluorescence lifetime (D).

The effects of different concentrations of glucose on the fluorescence spectra (A), (F 0-F)/F 0 (B), U-vis absorption spectra (C) and UV − vis absorbance (D) and Digital photos of the PPD-UCNPs solution in the presence of glucose with different concentrations ranging from 0 to 500 μM (E).
The application of the method for determination of serum samples with different amounts of glucose.

head.ce:title: A strategy for the accurate detection of glucose in human serum based on the IFE effect of up-transformed nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Technology Research Center on Reagent and Instrument for RAPID Detection of Product Quality and Food Safety, Fujian, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Technology Research Center on Reagent and Instrument for RAPID Detection of Product Quality and Food Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Technology Research Center on Reagent and Instrument for RAPID Detection of Product Quality and Food Safety, Fujian, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, the inner filter effect (IFE) was applied to construct a “turn off” mode up-conversion fluorescence and colorimetric dual-reading detection platform consisting of the chromogenic substance p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and NaYF4:Yb/Er@NaYF4 core–shell up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) for the detection of glucose and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with high sensitivity and selectivity. In this strategy, hydrogen peroxide is produced in the process of glucose oxidation by catalyzing glucose oxidase (GOx). PPD is oxidized by H2O2 under the action of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), resulting in the transformation into its oxidation product PPDox, whose absorption peak coincides with the emission peak of UCNP at 540 nm, which can significantly quench the fluorescence of UCNP. The quenching efficiency is related to PPDox production, so the relationship between the glucose content and the fluorescence changes of UCNP can be established to realize the sensitive detection of glucose. UCNPs modified by –COOH and the oxidation product of PPD were negatively charged, which increased the distance between donor and recipient due to electrostatic repulsion. It was not conducive to the occurrence of distance-dependent fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which was also verified by fluorescence lifetime test. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the linear ranges of hydrogen peroxide and glucose were 2.5 ∼ 50 and 5 ∼ 80 μmol/L with the detection limit (LOD) of 0.32 and 0.83 μmol/L, respectively, which were lower than of some reported approaches. Furthermore, the system could be applied to the detection of glucose in actual serum sample and the results were consistency of commercial glucose meters which indicated that the protocol was reliable and potential applications for clinical diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have shared the link to my date/code at the Attach File step

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose is the main energy source of cell activity and metabolic intermediates and the key substance of carbohydrate metabolism. Normal glucose levels in body fluids are essential for the cellular metabolic activity, and abnormal glucose levels can serve as a signal to diagnose diabetes. Therefore, it is indispensable to design a fast response and sensitive detection method for the diagnosis and treatment of diabetes mellitus [1].

Currently, methods used to detect glucose mainly include colorimetry [2,3], electrochemistry [4,5], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [6], chemiluminescence [7] and fluorometry [8]. Compared with other approaches, fluorescence method has been widely used to the analyze glucose levels in serum due to its advantages of sensitivity, simplicity and rapidity and luminescent materials used in fluorescence methods were organic dyes compounds [9], quantum dots (QDs) [10,11] and rare earth-doped upconversion nanoparticles [8]. Although the former has various kinds and advantages [12,13], compared with dyes compounds and QDs, as the third new fluorescent material, it offers significant advantages over organic dyes compounds and QDs, including high optical and chemical stability, low potential poisonousness, no background light interference and almost no damage to biological tissues [14–16]. Based on these advantages, UCNPs is of considerable interest for the detection of glucose from complex biological samples.

At present, the mechanisms of UCNPs to detect analytes are typically based on electron energy transfer (EET) [17], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [18,19] and photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [20,21], while detection for glucose mostly depended on FRET [22–24]. However, the prerequisite for FRET from UCNPs to accepters was a specific distance (less than 10 nm) between the two [25], which resulted in complex operations involving surface modifications of UCNPs energy donors or specially designed synthetic H2O2-responsive energy receptors to achieve FRET conditions by creating an interaction between the donor and the receptor after modification. For example, Zhang et al. developed a FRET system based on UCNPs and graphene oxide (GO) to sensitively detect glucose [24]. Concanavalin A (ConA) connected UCNPs were used as energy donors and chitosan (CS) linked GO was functioned as energy acceptors. FRET occurred when the UCNP is close enough to GO due to a specific binding between ConA and CS. In order to achieve this process, not only UCNP but also GO have undergone complex modifications, and strict experimental conditions such as time and pH are required. In addition, multiple purification and separation steps greatly extended the preparation time of the system, and the degree of modification directly affected the effect of FRET. As a non-irradiated energy conversion mode, the IFE was usually produced by the absorption of excitation light and/or emitted light from the absorber, therefore, there is no distance restriction between UCNPs and energy receptors. Generally, UCNPs for selective detection of glucose based on IFE consist of two steps. First, glucose and oxygen are converted to gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) by action of glucose oxidase (GOx). Then, under the action of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the obtained H2O2 oxidizes the chromogenic substrate to get its oxidation product which could quench the fluorescence of UCNPs because its absorption spectrum overlaps with the emission spectrum of UCNPs. Therefore, the method for the quantitative determination of glucose based on the above facts was constructed.

At present, the commonly used as chromogenic substrate are benzene derivatives, one of which is 3,3 ', 5,5 '-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) [26,27]. For example, Liu et al used UCNPs as the donor and TMB as the receptor to monitor glucose, and its LOD was 0.045 μmol/L with a linear range of 0.10 ∼ 5.0 μmol [22]. Another commonly used chromogenic substrate was p-phenylenediamine (PPD), whose oxidation products 2,5-diamino-N,N′-di-(4-aminophenyl)-2,5-cyclohexadiene-1,4-diimine (PPDox) have overlapping absorption peaks with UCNPs, which could strongly quench the fluorescence of UCNPs and cause changes in the absorbance value of the system [28,29]. Compared with the former, the water solubility of PPD was better than that of TMB, and the stability of the oxidation product PPDox was better than that of TMBox, which was conducive to accurate detection results [30,31]. However, UCNPs applied to detect glucose using PPD as the chromogenic substrate not only based on FRET but especially IFE have not been so far reported.

In this paper, a colorimetric fluorescence dual-mode detection strategy based on IFE using UCNPs as energy donors and the PPDox as energy acceptor for highly sensitive determination of glucose and hydrogen peroxide was proposed (Fig. 1 ). To certify the IFE occurrence of mechanism, the Zeta potential of UCNPs and PPDox, respectively, and the effect of the presence or absence of PPDox on the fluorescence lifetime of UCNPs were measured. In addition, serious key parameters as PPD, HRP and GOx concentrations as well as incubation time, temperature and pH value of HRP enzyme were also studied to obtain high sensitivity. Finally, the method was applied to detect glucose in real samples and the detection results were consistent with those of commercial glucose meter, indicating that this strategy had good reliability and practicability.

YCl3·6H2O (99.9 %), YbCl3·6H2O (99.9 %), ErCl3·6H2O (99.9 %), 1-octadecane (ODE, 90 %), oleic acid (OA, 95 %), ammonium fluoride (NH4F, 98 %), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), glucose oxidase (GOx), bovine albumin (BSA, 98 %), uric acid (UA, 99 %), lysine (Lys, 95 %), l-cystine (l-Cys, 99 %), galactose (99 %), fructose (99 %), xylose (98 %) were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. ltd (Shanghai, China). Cyclohexane, diethylene glycol (DEG, 98 %), chloroform, toluene (99.5 %), sucrose (AR), trisodium citrate, dihydrate, sodium hydroxide (96 %), absolute ethyl alcohol (AR) and methanol (AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. ltd (Shanghai, China). Glucose (AR) was purchased from Bellingway Technology Co. ltd. (Beijing, China). All chemicals are used directly and ultrapure water was used throughout.

Electron microscopic images of the size and morphology of UCNPs were obtained from transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200i, USA) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, SUPRA 55, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by an FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet 5700, USA). Upconversion luminescence spectra and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba jobin yvon, FluoroMax-4, France) and a Lambda 950 ultraviolet spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA), respectively. The crystalline phases of UCNPs was described with a X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Miniflex600, Japan). The zeta potentials of the surface charges of UCNPs and PPDox were determined by Zeta potential and nanoparticle size meter (Micromeritics Instruments ltd., NanoPlus 3, USA).

The UCNPs had been prepared by a solvothermal process, which was based on the reported procedure with some changes [32]. Briefly, 0.78 mmol of YCl3·6H2O, 0.2 mmol of YbCl3·6H2O and 0.02 mmol of ErCl3·6H2O were added to a three-necked flask containing 6 mL OA and 15 mL ODE. The water was removed by heating to 110 °C under argon atmosphere, and then heated to 160 °C for 30 min, until the rare earth chloride was completely dissolved. The solution was cooled to 50 °C and 10 mL of methanol solution dissolved with 4 mmol NH4F and 2.5 mmol sodium hydroxide was added to the reaction system and held for 30 min. Subsequently, the temperature was increased to 110 °C to remove the water and methanol in the solution. Then the mixed solution was rapidly heated up to 300 °C for 1.5 h. The reaction was cooled down to room temperature (RT), and the nanoparticles were collected by centrifugation after adding ethanol and settling. The product were rinsed three times with ethanol and cyclohexane, and stored in cyclohexane for subsequent experimental procedures.

Subsequently, the NaYF4: Yb, Er@NaYF4 core–shell nanoparticles (denoted as OA-UCNPs) synthesis method was adapted from the previously reported literature [33]. Briefly, 1 mmol YCI3·6H2O was added to a three-neck flask containing 15.0 mL of ODE and 6.0 mL of OA. The mixture was then warmed to 160 °C and held for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. After the solution was cooled to 80 °C, 2.0 mL of cyclohexane solution containing OA-UCNPs was added downward under a continuous flow of argon gas and reacted for 30 min. The following procedure was the same as previously described, followed by centrifugation and purification steps to obtain NaYF4:Yb,Er@NaYF4 core–shell UCNPs.

In accordance with the ligand exchange method previously reported, citric acid was used to treat monodisperse hydrophobic OA-UCNPs to introduce functional carboxyl groups and improve their water solubility [34]. Briefly, 2 mmol citric acid was added to a 15 mL DEG solution and heated to 110 °C for 30 min. When the solution was cooled to 50 °C, a mixture of chloroform (3 mL) and toluene (2 mL) dispersed with 25 mg OA-UCNPs was added and held for 30 min. Then, after the excess chloroform and toluene were removed, the temperature was raised to 180 °C and kept for 90 min (the whole reaction was carried out in an argon atmosphere). When the temperature was lowered to RT, anhydrous ethanol was added and citric acid-modified UCNPs (Cit-UCNPs) was collected by centrifugation. The precipitate was centrifuged 3 times by adding 10 mL ethanol and 5 mL ultrapure water, and the final product was dispersed in 5 mL ultrapure water for reserve.

Fluorescence spectrum measurement, first, 50 μL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 6.5), 50 μL of PPD (4 mmol/L), 50 μL of HRP (250 ng/mL) were mixed with 300 μL of a certain concentration of H2O2, after which 30 μL of UCNPs were added. The solution was immediately diluted to 500 μL with ultrapure water and incubated at 45 °C for 10 min. The upconversion fluorescence spectrum was measured under 980 nm excitation.

UV–vis spectroscopy measurement, twice the volume of the same concentration solution was successively added to a 4 mL centrifuge tube and shaken to achieve homogenize solution. Subsequently, they were incubated under the same experimental conditions. Ultimately, the final solution was transferred to a 1 cm quartz cuvette and its UV–vis absorption spectra were determined at RT.

Fluorescence spectrum measurement, first, 50 μL of PBS (pH = 6.5), 50 μL of PPD (4 mmol/L), 50 μL of HRP (250 ng/mL), 20 μL of GOx (50 μg/mL) were mixed with 300 μL of a certain concentration of glucose solution, which was then added with 30 μL of UCNPs. Subsequently, the solution was incubated under the same conditions and its upconverted fluorescence spectrum was measured at 980 nm excitation. UV–vis spectroscopy measurement, twice the volume of the same solution concentration was successively added to a 4 mL centrifuge tube and shaken to achieve homogenize solution. Subsequently, they were incubated under the same experimental conditions. Ultimately, the final solution was transferred to a 1 cm quartz cuvette and its UV–vis absorption spectra were determined at RT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Serum samples from four volunteers were collected from the Fuzhou NO.1 Hospital. The human serum sample was diluted 100 times in 20.0 mmol PBS (pH 7.4) prior to UCNPs detection of glucose. Then the design method was used for analysis, and the specific steps were the same as the above glucose detection steps. Finally, a certain concentration of glucose was added into the serum to detect the recovery, and each experiment was repeated three times.

The morphology and dimensions of UCNPs were characterized by FT-IR, XRD, SEM and TEM, and the results were describe in Fig. 2 . As can be seen from IR of OA-UCNPs in Fig. 2A (black line), the peaks at 3430 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibration of –OH from OA molecule while the two absorption peaks at 2931 and 2853 cm−1 were due to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the –CH2- from the long alkyl chain. And the absorption peaks around 1561 and 1465 cm−1 were caused by the asymmetry and symmetric stretching vibration of –COO-. Compared with OA-UCNPs, IR of Cit-UCNPs in Fig. 2A (red line) displayed that the peaks of –COOH were shifted to 1604 and 1406 cm−1, these results indicated that OA on the surface of nanoparticles was successfully replaced by –COOH ligand. Moreover, 3430 cm−1 corresponds to the –OH and 2934 and 2853 cm−1 corresponds to –CH2- from OA molecule became weak or even disappeared. According to the above analysis of FT-IR results, –COOH could have been modified on the UCNPs.

Fig. 2B showed the representative XRD pattern of the UCNPs in the 2θ range from 10 to 65°. The diffraction characteristics were 17.137, 29.960, 30.807, 43.472, 53.177 and 53.716°, which corresponded to the (100), (110), (101), (201), (300) and (211) facets of the cubic crystal structure of NaYF4, respectively (JCPDS no. 28–1192), demonstrating that the obtained UCNPs was high crystallinity, and no other impurities were observed. SEM (Fig. 2C) and TEM ( Fig. 2D) demonstrated that the achieved UCNPs were consistent in dimension and shape, and had good dispersion uniformity with the average diameter of 30 nm. Moreover, it could be clearly seen that the structure of UCNPs did not change before and after citrate acid modification UCNPs, and still showed a pure hexagonal structure with high crystallinity.

In this paper, based on the green emission peak (540 nm) of UCNPs, a highly sensitive and specific fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode detection method was established for the detection of glucose and H2O2 in human serum. The strategy's approach is based on the catalysis of glucose oxidase (GOx), which produces hydrogen peroxide during the oxidation of glucose. PPD is oxidized by H2O2 under the action of HRP enzyme, resulting in the conversion of its oxidation product PPDox. Because of the overlapping spectra of UCNPs and PPDox (Fig. 4A), they could be used as energy donors and acceptors, respectively. Therefore, its fluorescence could be significantly quenched by PPDox. Moreover, the effect of the absence of GOx, glucose, HRP and PPD on the two spectra of UCNPs were evaluated. As can be seen from Fig. 3
A and Fig. 3B, the emission peaks of UCNPs showed significant quenching only when glucose, GOx, HRP and PPD was simultaneously added into the reaction system (Fig. 3A). Likewise, the absorbance of the solution increased significantly (Fig. 3B). The results show that only when the above factors exist at the same time, the system will have obvious changes in fluorescence intensity and absorbance, therefore, this method was feasible to analyze H2O2 and then glucose by double-mode detection.

To illustrate the principle of UCNPs fluorescence quenching caused by PPDox, the UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of PPDox and UCNPs were first measured, respectively, and the results showed that there was effective spectral overlap between them (Fig. 4A), which was necessary to achieve quenching [35]. Subsequently, the UV–vis absorption spectrums of PPDox solutions were investigated before and after the addition of UCNPs (Fig. 4B). The absorption bands position and intensity of PPDox did not change after the addition of UCNPs, which certified that there was no compound between UCNPs (energy donors) and PPDox (energy acceptor) by chemical interaction [36]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of PPDox to UCNPs was attributed the IFE, and similar reports were seen in the literatures [37].

To substantiate the principle, the zeta potentials of the surface charges of the two substances were measured (Fig. 4C). Test consequence indicate that the zeta potential of UCNPs changed from positive to negative potentials after ligand exchange, indicating that the ligand exchange successfully attached –COOH to UCNPs. Secondly, the zeta potentials of Cit-UCNPs and PPDox were −49.1 mV and −17.6 mV, respectively, indicating that the distance between PPDox and UCNPs should be greater than 10 nm due to electrostatic repulsion, which was also reported in the literature [38]. Therefore, FRET could not occur between PPDox and UCNPs. To further confirm this mechanism, the effect of PPDox (energy acceptor) on the fluorescence lifetime of UCNPs (energy donors) was also studied. As described in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence lifetime curves of UCNPs in the presence and absence of PPDox were almost completely overlapped, which was consistent with IFE mechanism. Therefore, the above discussion indicated that the fluorescence of UCNPs was quenched by PPDox probably through the IFE mechanism rather than the FRET mechanism [39].

For the purpose of a sensitive response for the glucose assay, several key parameters such as the concentrations of HRP, PPD, GOx, as well as the optimal pH, temperature and reaction time of the enzymatic were optimized. The fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F 0 − F)/F 0) was utilized as a criterion for selecting the optimal conditions, where F 0 denoted fluorescence intensity in the absence of glucose and F was the opposite.

The different concentrations of PPD (0 ∼ 16 mmol/L), HRP (0 ∼ 500 ng/mL) and GOx (0 ∼ 200 μg/mL) were optimized. As describe in Fig. S1
(A, B and C), the fluorescence quenching efficiency of UCNPs was relatively weak when the concentrations of PPD, HRP and GOx were relatively lower which may be due to small amount of PPDox generated or inadequate contact between the enzyme and other reactants. And the (F 0 − F)/F 0 attained the greatest value when the concentrations of PPD, HRP and GOx were 4 mmol/L, 250 ng/mL and 50 μg/mL, which were chosen above concentrations as the experimental conditions for subsequent optimization.

Moreover, the effect of incubation duration (0 ∼ 30 min) was also studied under the optimal conditions mentioned above. The effect of time in Fig. S1 D show, the (F 0 − F)/F 0 increased rapidly with time, and the (F 0 − F)/F 0 almost increased to the highest when the incubation duration was 1 min. The experimental results indicated that the reaction between PPD and H2O2 was very fast, and most of the PPDox can be produced in a short time. After 10 min, the (F 0 − F)/F 0 of the system was not changing indicating that the H2O2 was fully involved in the reaction. Therefore, 10 min was the best incubation time.

Similarly, the impact of temperature (25 ∼ 60 ℃) for (F 0 − F)/F 0 was investigated under the optimized reaction time of 10 min. As shown in Fig. S1 E, the (F 0 − F)/F 0 elevated when the temperatures were enhanced from 25 to 45 ℃ which was probably due to the increase of reaction rate. And the (F 0 − F)/F 0 reached the peak when the temperature reached 45 ℃. However, when the temperature continued to rise, the fluorescence quenching efficiency decreased, which might be because the temperature exceeded the optimal temperature of the enzyme, leading to the enzyme inactivation. According to the experimental results, 45 ℃ was chosen as the optimal temperature of incubation. Finally, the production of PPDox depends on the synergistic effect of GOx and HRP, so the incubation temperature of GOx enzyme was no longer optimized separately, and 45 ℃ was chosen as the incubation temperature for both.

Normally, weakly acidic conditions favor the catalytic decomposition of H2O2, therefore, the effect of pH values (4.0 ∼ 8.0) was explored regarding the fluorescence quenching. As describe in Fig. S1 F, the quenching efficiency showed an upward trend and the change gradually accelerated within the pH range of 4.0 to 6.5. When pH = 6.5, the fluorescence quenching efficiency achieved the maximum, which was the result of the enzyme pH value gradually approaching the optimal pH value of the enzyme, so that the catalytic activity of the enzyme reached the maximum value. When pH exceeded 6.5, the quenching efficiency decreased, which was caused by the enzyme losing its activity. Therefore, pH 6.5 of the buffer solution was utilized for subsequent steps.

Under optimum conditions, the fluorescence spectral response of H2O2 (0 ∼ 500 μmol/L) by this method at different concentration levels was studied (Fig. S2 A). When the fluorescence intensity of the system decreased gradually when the different concentrations of H2O2 (0 ∼ 500 μmol/L) were added. The calibration line was constructed by plotting the (F 0 − F)/F 0 against the H2O2 concentration. Fig. S2 B shows that there were good linear relationships between (F 0 − F)/F 0 and H2O2 concentrations which were in range of 2.5 ∼ 50 μmol/L (R 2 = 0.9927) and 50 ∼ 300 μmol/L (R 2 = 0.9969), respectively. The LODs of H2O2 was calculated as 0.32 μmol/L (S/N = 3), which had a lower LOD than those reported for H2O2 by some other assays ( Table S1 ).

Simultaneously, the absorption response of UV–vis absorption spectroscopy to the change of H2O2 concentration was describe in Fig. S2 C, D (Fig. S2E shows the apparent change in color from colorless to purple). The absorbance of the sensing system at 525 nm was significantly strengthened with the increase of H2O2 content and the absorbance values (within 0 ∼ 500 μmol/L) was linearly correlated with H2O2 content. The LODs obtained by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was calculated to be 1.07 μmol/L.

The fluorescence spectral response of glucose (0 ∼ 400 μmol/L) by this method at different concentration levels was studied (Fig. 5 A, B). The fluorescence intensity of the system positively correlated with the glucose concentration and the linear response range was 5 ∼ 80 μmol/L(R2 = 0.997) and 80 ∼ 300 μmol/L(R2 = 0.996), respectively. The calibration curve could be indicated by F = 0.122 + 0.0029X and F = 0.235 + 0.0016X, respectively. The LOD calculated from the calibration curve was 0.83 μmol/L (S/N = 3), which had a lower LOD than those reported for glucose by some other assays ( Table S2 ).

In addition, the absorption response of UV–vis absorption spectroscopy to the change of glucose concentration was describe in Fig. 5C, D (Fig. 5E shows the apparent change in color from colorless to purple). It shows that the absorbance was linearly correlated with the glucose concentration (0 ∼ 500 μmol/L) and the LOD value of glucose detection can be obtained from the calibration curve as 1.22 μmol/L.

To test the specificity of this system for glucose, the response of the detection system to potential interfering substances in the sample was measured. It can be seen from Fig. S3 that only glucose could significantly cause fluorescence quenching of UCNPs, while the concentration of other substances has almost no effect on the intensity of UCNPs even though it was 10 times more concentrated than glucose (5 mmol/L). The results obviously indicated that the response of the sensor to interfering substances was significantly lower than that to glucose, so it has good selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose monitoring in real serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: For verification and evaluation the practicality and accuracy of this method, real and spiked serum samples were detected using this method and the results were compared with those of a commercial blood glucose meter (Table 1 ). Moreover, the recoveries calculated by standard addition experiments were 99.5 %-109 % with relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was less than 5 %. These results indicated that the sensor system has superior ability to detect glucose in complex serum samples.

Altogether, we have designed a facile and effective fluorescence nanosensor for the accurate detection of glucose in serum based on IFE mechanism between UCNPs and PPDox oxidized from PPD. The morphology and dimensions of modified UCNPs were described by TEM, SEM, FT-IR and XRD. The feasibility and detection mechanism of the assay was discussed in detail. Some key experimental parameters were optimized and under optimal experimental conditions the linear range of detection glucose were 5–80 μmol/L with LODs of 0.83 μmol/L, which were lower than of some reported approaches. This method was further used to detect glucose in serum samples and validated with the results of a commercial glycameter. In summary, the strategy proposed in this protocol is highly selective and sensitive determination of glucose and can also be used for future determination of other biometabolites involved in chemical reactions related to hydrogen peroxide production or consumption.

Tianlu Zhao: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yangqing Li: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xuecheng Zhang: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Haixia Lyu: Writing – review & editing. Zenghong Xie: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21307013), Science and Technology program of Fujian Province (2019Y0003) and Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2015J01044).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108363.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A high-throughput colorimetric assay for glucose detection based on glucose oxidase-catalyzed enlargement of gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. M. Xiong, Y. Y. Zhang, P. F. Rong, J. Yang, W. Wang, D. B. Liu, A high-throughput colorimetric assay for glucose detection based on glucose oxidase-catalyzed enlargement of gold nanoparticles, Nanoscale 7 (2015) 15584-15588. doi: 10.1039/C5NR03758A.

Chen, Immobilized Ferrous Ion and Glucose Oxidase on Graphdiyne and Its Application on One-Step Glucose Detection, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 11 (2019) 2647-2654. doi: 10.1021/acsami.8b03118.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chen, X. Hai, X. W. Chen, J. H. Wang, In-situ Growth of Silver Nanoparticles on Graphene Quantum Dots for Ultra-sensitive Colorimetric Detection of H2O2 and Glucose, Anal. Chem. 86 (2014) 6689-6694. doi: 10.1021/ac501497d.
